content.text: Task framing is the choice of how to present a problem to the model — what surface the model is answering on, what role it inhabits, and what the task is named. The same underlying problem can be framed as a classification task, a completion task, a Q&A task, or an extraction task, and each framing activates a different learned pattern in the model's weights. Because language models learned from text written by humans for humans, framing that matches a familiar human writing context generally produces better results than abstract or unusual framings.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def ask(prompt: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=100,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


TEXT = "The battery drains in 2 hours. The screen is vivid and bright."

# Same task, four different frames — notice how depth and format shift

# Frame 1: Abstract instruction
print("[Abstract]")
print(ask(f"Perform sentiment analysis on: {TEXT}"))
print()

# Frame 2: Classification task (Q&A format)
print("[Q&A Classification]")
print(ask(f"Is the following review Positive, Negative, or Mixed?\n{TEXT}\nAnswer:"))
print()

# Frame 3: Expert reviewer frame
print("[Expert reviewer]")
print(ask(
    f"You are a product analyst. Identify the positive and negative aspects "
    f"of this review in two bullet points each:\n{TEXT}"
))
print()

# Frame 4: Structured extraction frame
print("[Extraction]")
print(ask(
    f"Extract pros and cons from this review as JSON with keys 'pros' and 'cons':\n{TEXT}"
))
Standard tasks — the model knows what the verb means
Shifts depth and vocabulary toward a domain
When you want the output to read as a specific document type
Force the model to challenge its first answer

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def ask(prompt: str, stop: list[str] | None = None) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=60,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=stop or [],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


claims = [
    "Python is the most popular programming language.",
    "The Great Wall of China is visible from space.",
    "Vitamin C cures the common cold.",
]

# Frame A: Free-form fact-check — produces varied, verbose output
print("Frame A (free-form):")
for claim in claims:
    print(f"  {ask(f'Is this true? {claim}')[:60]}")
print()

# Frame B: Fill-in-the-blank — forces a single consistent label
print("Frame B (fill-in-the-blank):")
for claim in claims:
    label = ask(
        f'The claim "{claim}" is: TRUE / FALSE / DISPUTED\nAnswer:',
        stop=["\n", "."]
    )
    print(f"  {label:10s}  {claim}")
print()

# Frame C: Binary Q&A — simplest, most parseable
print("Frame C (binary Q&A):")
for claim in claims:
    answer = ask(
        f'Q: Is this claim accurate? "{claim}"\nAnswer (Yes or No):',
        stop=["\n"]
    )
    print(f"  {answer:5s}  {claim}")
Match the frame to a document type the model has seen.

Academic papers, code reviews, meeting notes, customer support tickets — these are all text genres with predictable structure. Framing a task as one of these genres activates the corresponding patterns.
"The sentiment is ___" forces a single token continuation. It is more reliable than "What is the sentiment?" which invites a sentence.
Use Q&A format to constrain length.
Ending a prompt with "A:" (or "Answer:") signals that a short, direct answer is expected. This competes with the model's default tendency toward verbose explanation.
Test multiple frames on the same task.
Frame sensitivity is task-dependent — the best frame is not predictable a priori. Run 3–4 variants on a sample of inputs and pick the one with most consistent output before deploying.

Framing is the least-appreciated dimension of prompt engineering. Two prompts that ask for the same underlying output can produce radically different results because they activate different learned patterns — the model does not process meaning abstractly, it processes text statistically. Choosing a frame is choosing which part of the training distribution to land in. The best frames are those that map the task onto a genre the model has seen many high-quality examples of during training.
